ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a cauliflower food product class (FOODON_03301710); no specific wok-fried or stir-fried cauliflower subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents cauliflower as a raw commodity. No specific stir-fried or wok-fried cauliflower preparation code exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226723007 represents cauliflower as a substance. No specific prepared/cooked cauliflower dish code exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of wok-fried organic cauliflower as a side dish in a Singaporean Chinese rice bowl meal is approximately 150 g, representing a generous portion of florets with seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Cauliflower, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11136); adapted for stir-fried preparation with oil addition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked cauliflower (NDB 11136, FDC ID 169986) used as the base. Values adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for wok-frying with approximately 5–8 g vegetable oil per 100 g cooked dish, and sodium adjusted for soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese stir-fry preparations. Protein and carbohydrate values retained from USDA cooked cauliflower baseline. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Cauliflower, GI value from non-starchy vegetable category — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for stir-fried preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cauliflower is a non-starchy vegetable with a GI of approximately 15 (low), consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy vegetables. Wok-frying with oil does not materially alter the GI of cauliflower as the carbohydrate content remains low. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (15 × 7.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.69, rounded to 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Wok-fried cauliflower prepared with soy sauce contributes approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 7.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, wok-fried organic cauliflower has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting blood glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high fibre, and presence of beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wok-fried organic cauliflower is low in saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre (2.5 g per 100 g) and vitamin C (35 mg per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; however, sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wok-fried organic cauliflower is a Chinese-style stir-fried vegetable dish prepared by cooking organic cauliflower florets in a hot wok with vegetable oil, garlic, soy sauce, and optional oyster sauce. The high-heat wok technique produces florets that are lightly charred on the exterior while remaining tender-crisp inside, with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile from the soy-based seasoning. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a side vegetable accompaniment in Chinese rice bowl and bento-style meals, as offered by restaurants such as First Bowl Rice Kitchen. Nutritionally, wok-fried organic cauliflower is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable dish providing approximately 85 kcal per 100 g, with 2.0 g protein, 5.5 g fat (primarily from cooking oil), and 7.5 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of dietary fibre (2.5 g per 100 g), vitamin C (35 mg per 100 g), folate (44 mcg DFE per 100 g), and potassium (210 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking low-GI dietary patterns. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content (approximately 280 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The use of organic cauliflower implies reduced pesticide exposure but does not materially alter the nutritional profile compared to conventionally grown cauliflower.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried cauliflower?
Is stir-fried cauliflower good for diabetics?
Is organic cauliflower stir-fry low carb?
Can I eat wok-fried cauliflower if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: side dish in Chinese rice bowl meal

confidence_reason: No exact match for wok-fried organic cauliflower exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked cauliflower (FDC ID 169986) with adjustments for wok-frying oil addition and soy sauce sodium. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked cauliflower with upward adjustment for fat and energy (wok-frying oil ~5–8 g per 100 g cooked dish) and sodium (soy sauce seasoning). This is an estimation; values may vary by restaurant preparation. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as cauliflower is a plant food containing no vitamin B12; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as cauliflower contains negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oils used in stir-frying contain negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for typical vegetable oils. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base preparation; naturally zero. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6), potassium_mg (210.0), calcium_mg (22.0), iron_mg (0.5), vitamin_c_mg (35.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (1.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.05), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.06), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0), folate_mcg_dfe (44.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.4), phosphorus_mg (44.0), magnesium_mg (15.0), zinc_mg (0.3), selenium_mcg (0.6), and water_g (83.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cauliflower data adapted for stir-fried preparation. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for cauliflower as a non-starchy vegetable per University of Sydney GI Database conventions. 8. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false as oyster sauce is commonly used in Chinese restaurant preparations; is_vegetarian set to true for the base cauliflower preparation without oyster sauce. 9. Allergen note: soy and gluten allergens present from soy sauce; individuals with coeliac disease or soy allergy should confirm preparation method with restaurant.
